# Transcrição: 3. Case Study- Using Offline Conversion Data To Scale to $200k spend

- **Fonte:** 3. Case Study- Using Offline Conversion Data To Scale to $200k spend
- **Duração:** 11:26
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

So now that you understand the importance of offline conversion data being pulled into Ads Manager, and I've explained to you, hopefully, the need for it, let me show you an actual use case with an account and what difference it has made to our analysis. So if I just take one campaign, so I'm just going to highlight this one campaign just to show you what happens when you bring offline conversion data in. So 33k spent, and you look at this column here. This is website purchase ROAS. This is based on the purchase pixel. This is what most people will be using in their ROAS analysis. Then you look at the column next to it, this is purchase ROAS. This takes your pixel data, plus your offline conversion data, and then applies the ROAS. What we're seeing here is the difference between just relying on the pixel and also bringing in offline conversions. So just to recap for you, the offline conversions will bring data from your backend sales tool, in this case from Shopify. And what it's doing is when we plug that data from Shopify back into Ads Manager, Facebook is trying to fill gaps in its own reporting. So what it does is to say, all right, we know that 33k of spend went into this one campaign, and we directly saw through the pixel that we had a 0.69 return. However, we then plug additional data in, and what Facebook does is it looks at the data, the timestamp, the email address, the user, all that kind of stuff. And if it finds a match on a click based on something that it identifies, in most cases the email address could be the timestamp of the purchase, could be many other things in between. But if Facebook finds a match to that campaign, to that click, then it attributes it to that campaign. And you can see here, that's changed the whole aspect of this particular campaign. And I know this because we discussed this many times with this client about making sure we're using the right data. So I'll give you a different look on this, for example. This is a level four campaign for us, which is existing customers. 18k spent, pixel shows a 1.87 ROAS, which for an existing customer campaign is not very good. But when we plug in all of the missed data, we get 4.8. Another one here, add testing, 11k spent, 2x ROAS versus 4.0 something. Again here, warm campaign, 2x ROAS versus a 7x ROAS. You can see the numbers here. So this is the purchase conversion value. This is taking all of the revenue, which is pixel data plus your offline conversion data. I'm giving you the total value.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

So what we're seeing here is 33k spent and actually 55k generated. If we did not have offline conversion data to upload, all we would see would be this column, which is 22k. This is where incorrect decision making is made. Now what that looks like for your data, I don't know, because it could be that your pixel is collecting more data, could also be you're collecting less. Now why does data not get collected in this client's case? Because we've been working with them for about three years now. I know that, so they're an apparel e-commerce store. I know from past analysis that on average, their ROAS doubles two weeks after the first click. So if from the first click, sorry, the first day, so if the first day ROAS for any part of their campaign is, let's say 1x, we know that in 14 days it will go to 2x. Or if it's at 2x, it will go to 4x. And in some cases, higher than that as well. So we've known this for some time that because there's a delay from click to purchase in the way that Facebook used to work, it will get attributed later on. So we'd be confident of scaling at certain ROAS numbers, let's say 1, 1.5, 2x on the day, even though it wasn't profitable if we looked at it just at those numbers, knowing that the traffic we're driving today, and this is really, really important, I want you to take this away, the traffic we are driving today is going to convert to sale in the next seven to 14 days if it doesn't convert today. Now if you fast forward to when we are looking at this data, you can see the date on the screen, this is the post iOS 14 world. In that world, we only have seven days of tracking because the seven day click attribution is now in effect. There's no 28 day, you can't go beyond seven day right now. So that means, and this is specifically for this account and it will also apply for many of you as well. If someone clicks and purchases eight days later, well guess what your campaign doesn't get attributed with that sale. However, if you upload offline conversion data, then Facebook do attribute it. And this is the cool thing about offline conversion data is when it comes to attribution, they're not bound by the seven day pixel window. So a lot of these conversions may well have happened outside of that seven day window and the offline conversion data is pulling it in. So here we have a total revenue of 55K of which only 22K was recorded on the pixel and 33K was recorded offline. That's a lot of potential revenue to lose out on if you're not using offline conversions. We can then see it by the purchases actually made. So 536 in total, 242 were through the pixel that were tracked through the pixel. 294 were not captured by the pixel. So if we had no offline conversion, we'd be making very different decisions on this campaign.

## [06:02 - 09:06]

We can see on conversion rate. So I've created two custom metrics here, which I won't go into detail on how to create custom metrics. You can search that on Facebook. But what I've done for this first column here is to take the total purchases that have come through, so this 536 and then divide it by the number of clicks. That gives me a 2.9% conversion rate. However, if I take the 242 divided by clicks, that gives me a lower conversion rate. So if I'm only looking at my pixel data, I'm looking at this and thinking, I know my site average is 2 to 2.5%. Oh, this campaign is doing terrible. It's 1.2%. It's not. We're just missing the data. So this is where you need to really understand what's going on. Here, AOV and AOV2. AOV1 is based on pixel-based purchases. AOV2 is based on overall purchases. And again, we see if we tally up all of the data, we're actually getting a higher AOV. So just to kind of recap here, without offline conversion data, and this is not to say it's going to have an astounding impact on every single person that applies it. But what I'm saying is make sure that you install offline conversions, and then you also measure your data in a similar way to see the impact. For some of you, it may not be as big, especially if, let's say you're selling products at a low ticket and it's, you know, a lot of people buy on day one or click one or things like that. So you might not have a lag and a delay and everything's captured in your seven days. But don't also forget that we're in a space where pixel data is being challenged. G blockers, Chrome updates, Google versus Apple versus Facebook, this pixel data I imagine is going to get worse and worse and worse. So how do you counter that conversion API is one of them. So you're trying to get that data through from the from the platform, the from your web server, I in this case Shopify. But as you can see here, this is using Kapi already. And yet we are still losing so much data. And that's the power of having offline conversions to be able to pull the missing data back in. So, you know, I think the big misunderstanding here is that conversion API is going to solve so many problems. It does not. It solves specific problems when the pixel cannot capture the data. But there are also occasions where also the server API, the server picks, well, let's not call it pixel, the server API is also not collecting the data. And this is the prime example where Facebook are limited to that seven days and you can't see that data. So conversion API plus offline conversions. That is, I believe one of the biggest takeaways you should take from this. Now you might be looking at this and saying, Hey, you know, I don't have an e-com store. I do lead Jen or I do, you know, telephone sales and things like that. Well, guess what? You can also push offline conversions through for telephone calls and things like that. In fact, there's a very popular third party tracking software, which I know people are

## [09:06 - 11:26]

quite amazed when it was able to bring call tracking into your ads manager and things like that. Guess what? It's just using offline conversion data. And at some other point, I'll show you how to do that for telephone sales and lead Jen and things like that. However, offline conversions are so underrated and make sure that you do at least give it a chance. Do it manually. If you can't automate it, if you can automate it as this client has done, you can do it through Zapier with Shopify. You can do it through Zapier with ClickFunnels, with WooCommerce. Like I'm not an advocate of Zapier. I might as well be the other platforms out there as well. But it's not difficult. You just need to follow the guides and I'll share some links below on the section on how to get those offline conversions. But this is a prime use case for this that if you want to make the right decisions with your campaigns, then you have to give the ads manager as much data as possible. This is even without looking at Google Analytics, without looking at all the click path data and things like that. You can see the differences in return on ad spend here. It's astounding. It's astonishing. And once you plug that data in, it doesn't matter what you're spending. This is a high spend account. You could be spending $2, $3K a month. You could still be losing $100, $200, $300 in your tracking. That could really knock your decision making off because it would be very easy to turn something off and say, this campaign is not performing because we expect better for our existing customers and therefore we're going to pause it and do something else. When in reality, it's pretty much a 5X ROAS campaign. Some of these campaigns, especially this one, like this one is a warm mid funnel campaign. It's actually doing better than our main campaign overall. However, we're not looking at a campaign by campaign. We look at this layer by layer. The decision here would be put more money in because it's a 7X ROAS. Maybe do some optimizations here. And with the same reporting, you can click into the ad set and the ad and drill down and see the exact same data all the way down to the ad level. So your decision making is better rounded. And once you start to use this alongside Google Analytics and alongside looking at data in Shopify, you'll be able to better navigate your dollar to give you the best return.
